exemplar.title: An exploration of the Baddeley and Hitch Word Length Effect: A partial replication investigating the length of syllabic words and ability to recall them.

detailedExemplarData.title: An exploration of the Baddeley and Hitch Word Length Effect: A partial replication investigating the length of syllabic words and ability to recall them.

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement of the aim linked to high-school learning strategies and relevance to educators
Accurate description of the Working Memory Model and explicit connection to the Word Length Effect
Correct identification and operationalisation of independent and dependent variables
weaknesses: Aim paragraph is incomplete and lacks full explanation of how recall is measured
Minor inaccuracies in theory description (e.g., citation formatting)
Hypotheses could more precisely restate operational definitions and include effect-size expectations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the introduction meets most top-band descriptors by stating an aim, explaining its relevance, describing the theory, and operationalising variables (all evidence of high-level work), the incomplete aim paragraph and minor theoretical inaccuracies prevent a full 5â6 band award. Reducing one mark to 4 reflects these weaknesses while acknowledging overall strong performance.

strengths: Thorough explanation of the repeated-measures design, including control of individual differences and counterbalancing
Detailed ethical procedures and consistent procedure timing enhancing internal validity
Clear description of controlled variables and materials used
weaknesses: No rationale for sample size or normality of demographic balance
Environmental controls (noise, lighting) are not justified
The 5-minute break counterbalancing lacks justification from literature or pilot data
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section demonstrates an explained design and well-implemented controls, meriting upper-band recognition. However, omitted rationale for sample size and environmental controls prevents a full top mark. Awarding 3 out of 4 balances strengths and these gaps.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive and inferential statistics with clear p-value reporting and hypothesis linkage
Selection of a Wilcoxon test shows understanding of non-parametric methods
Descriptive statistics (means, SDs) calculated correctly and interpreted in relation to the hypothesis
weaknesses: Wilcoxon test misnamed and no normality check conducted
Graphical presentation contains minimal axis labels, unexplained error bars, and incomplete title
Effect size is not reported, and justification for statistical choices is lacking
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis applies correct statistical methods and interprets findings at a high level. Persistent technical errors (misnaming tests, missing normality assessment) and graphing flaws justify a reduction from top band. A score of 4 fairly represents this mix.

strengths: Insightful discussion linking findings to the phonological loop and cognitive load theory
Strengths and limitations are relevant, stated, and clearly explained in context
Modifications are explicitly linked to limitations and fully justified
weaknesses: Some confusion between reliability and validity concepts in limitations
One proposed modification needs literature justification for benchmark values
Minor coherence issues in linking stratified sampling suggestion directly to its limitation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation demonstrates deep engagement with theory, thorough explanation of strengths/limitations, and well-justified modifications. Minor conceptual confusions and suggestions for improved coherence prevent a top-band mark but warrant a strong 5 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph comparing mean recalls is provided and directly relates to the hypothesis.  Nevertheless, axis labels and units are minimal, error bars are unexplained, and the title is cut off, so presentation errors exist.  That aligns with the 3â4 band with errors; 3 is appropriate.

The student states the statistical outcome (p = 0.00014), explicitly rejects the null hypothesis, and links the direction of difference to the descriptive data and the word-length theory.  Interpretation aligns findings with hypothesis and data.  Minor depth issues (no effect size) suggest a score of 5 within the top band.

Both null and research hypotheses are stated.  IV (word length) and DV (number of words correctly recalled) are named and operationalised (one- vs five-syllable lists; count of correctly ordered recalls).  Because operationalisation is explicit, the top band is met; wording is clear though concise, warranting 5.

Opportunity sampling is described and its practicality, time efficiency, and avoidance of psychology students are explained, along with demographic balance.  These reasons constitute an explanation rather than description, satisfying the upper band.  Minor omissions about sample size justification keep the score at 4.

The Working Memory Model is described with its four components and emphasis on the phonological loop; the Word Length Effect is also outlined.  The student then explains precisely how presenting mono- versus penta-syllabic word lists tests this mechanism.  The linkage between theory and procedure is therefore explicit and accurate, fulfilling the 5â6 band; minor inaccuracies (e.g., citation formatting) justify awarding 5 rather than 6.

Findings are related to the phonological loop and cognitive load, explaining how the results corroborate Baddeley & Hitch.  The student interprets SD differences and links them conceptually, demonstrating discussion rather than simple description.  This satisfies the 5â6 band; clarity but limited depth warrants 5.

The student clearly states the aim â to examine whether word length influences recall â and situates it within the practical context of high-school learning strategies.  They go beyond merely naming a purpose by explaining why the effect matters for educators and students, linking potential applications (chunking information, test design) to the investigation.  This explicit, applied explanation satisfies the descriptor for the top band, although the rationale could be more detailed, so a mid-band score of 5 is appropriate.

Multiple relevant strengths (participant control, standardised materials) and limitations (fatigue, ecological validity, sampling bias) are stated and each is given an explanatory sentence showing relevance to the investigation.  This meets âstated and explainedâ; therefore a score of 5 in the top band is appropriate.

Controlled variables (identical word lists, timing, pens down) and materials (tables, A4 sheets, vocabulary level) are given with some rationale (equal opportunity, minimise language confounds).  The explanation is present but thinner than for other strands, and omissions such as room conditions reduce completeness, so 3 is awarded within the top band.

The student proposes clear modificationsâusing stratified sampling to address representativeness and reducing writing time to counter rehearsalâexplicitly linking each to a corresponding limitation and justifying the expected improvement.  This fulfils the requirement for explicit linkage and full justification, meriting band 5â6; a score of 5 is assigned.

The student not only identifies a repeated-measures design but explains why it was chosen (controls participant variables) and acknowledges order effects with a counterbalancing break.  This explanatory depth meets the 3â4 band; the justification, while sound, is brief, so 4 is appropriate.

Means, medians, modes and SDs are calculated, and a Wilcoxon test is appliedâan appropriate non-parametric test for repeated measures.  However, it is misnamed as a rank-sum rather than signed-rank test, and no check of normality is reported.  These technical inaccuracies place the work in the âappropriate but contains errorsâ band; a mid-score of 3 is justified.

annotations.comment: Graph title references Google Sheets but lacks detail on variables; revise to âMean number of words recalled by syllable lengthâ for clarity and context.
The limitation of opportunity sampling is noted, but linking the suggestion of stratified sampling directly to this limitation in the same section would improve coherence.
Proposal to use stratified sampling directly addresses representativeness limitation and is fully justified, showing clear linkage between modification and limitation.

The introduction effectively grounds the investigation in the Working Memory Model, outlining its components and highlighting the phonological loopâs role, which shows a strong theoryâmethod connection.
Justification of the repeated-measures design highlights control over individual differences and internal validity, demonstrating solid research design understanding.
The aim is stated and relevance to high school learning is introduced, but the paragraph is cut off, lacking a full explanation of how recall will be measured; clarify the complete aim.
The introduction connects the investigation to educational strategies, explaining why understanding word length effects can inform teaching methods, which enhances the rationale.
Variables are correctly identified and operationalized, but specifying how syllable counts were verified (e.g., phonetic dictionary) would strengthen clarity.
The null hypothesis is clearly stated but could be improved by restating the operational definitions of IV and DV to ensure precise hypothesis wording.
The research hypothesis states the expected direction clearly but would be more robust by indicating an anticipated effect size or meaningful difference threshold.
Participant demographics (age, gender, language background) are detailed, but there is no rationale for the sample size of 21; consider discussing power or practical constraints.
Ethical procedures (informed consent, debriefing, confidentiality) are well implemented, ensuring participant rights and transparency.
Procedure timing is consistent across participants, reinforcing internal validity. For further rigor, specify how timing was monitored (e.g., stopwatch, automatic alert).
Counterbalancing with a 5-minute break is noted, but the choice of a 5-minute interval is not justified; provide rationale based on pilot data or literature.
The sampling technique is described and demand characteristic avoidance is explained, yet discussion of generalizability to other populations is missing.
Controlled variables are listed, but environmental controls (noise level, lighting, seating) are not described; include justification to reduce confounds.
Materials are described, but justification for selecting A1âB1 level vocabulary is missing; reference the CEFR or pilot data to support this choice.
Good selection of descriptive statistics; however, justification for using mean and SD given potential non-normal distribution is absent. Consider reporting the median if data are skewed.
Table presents means and standard deviations clearly, but the headers lack units (words recalled out of 8). Include explicit units to avoid ambiguity.
Interpretation that tighter clustering reflects uniform performance is insightful, but link this explicitly to the hypothesis on word-length effects to strengthen interpretation.
Bar graph effectively shows mean differences, but axis labels are minimal and error bars lack definition (SD vs. SE); clarify these elements in the legend or caption.
Inferential analysis correctly uses a nonparametric test, yet the Wilcoxon is misnamed (should be signed-rank) and no normality check is reported; address these inaccuracies.
The evaluation discusses how results support the phonological loop and Word Length Effect, demonstrating strong integration of findings with theory.
Strengths and limitations section identifies key points, but here it confuses reliability with validity; clarify these concepts to avoid misinterpretation.
Reducing recall time to 20 seconds is practical, but the 20-second benchmark needs justificationâreference pilot observations or relevant literature for this change.
The conclusion restates significance but reports Pâ<â0.001 differently than earlier pâ=â0.00014; maintain consistent p-value reporting throughout the report.
